questions.answer: AD 2024-01-02 supersedes AD-2022.
AD 2020-05-11 is fully superseded by this AD.
The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) mandated FAA-AD-2024-01-02.
FAA AD 2024-01-02 applies to the Boeing 737 MAX aircraft family, specifically including the 737-9 model.
The Boeing 737 MAX is manufactured by Boeing.
The Boeing 737 MAX is equipped with the CFM LEAP-1B engine.

MCAS Software v2.0 is the current, mandated version that supersedes v1.0. It is required by the effective regulation FAA AD 2024-01-02, which replaces all earlier directives that governed previous versions. The key technical advancement in v2.0 is the AOA Sensor Fusion Enhancement, which uses a Kalman-filter-based sensor fusion algorithm.
Southwest Airlines, United Airlines, American Airlines, Ryanair, and Air Canada operate Boeing 737 MAX aircraft.
FAA AD 2024-01-02 supersedes AD 2022-03-07 and AD 2020-05-11. AD 2022-03-07 partially superseded AD 2020-05-11. AD 2020-05-11 superseded AD 2020-05-11.

The context does not contain information describing a regulatory path from the FAA to a maintenance crew for the CFM LEAP-1B engine. It only notes that Boeing holds FAA Production Approval and Design Approval Holder status under 14 CFR Parts 21, 25, and 39, and lists various FAA Airworthiness Directives applicable to the 737 MAX. No details about communication or delegation to maintenance crews are provided.

The compliance process was governed by FAA AD 2020-05-11, which mandated requirements for the aircraft's return to service. For Southwest Airlines' Boeing 737 MAX 8 fleet, compliance actions included installation of MCAS v2.0 as specified in Boeing Service Bulletin SB 737-31-1944, Rev 2, superseding MCAS v1.7.3. The work also involved activation of the AOA Disagree alert system, performed by SR Technics Zurich. The aircraft returned to revenue service on March 11, 2021.

The 2022 directive replaced the 2020 directive and has since been superseded by the 2024 directive. For current operators, the 2022 directive is no longer the effective regulation. They must comply with the latest 2024 directive, which mandates updated software requirements that supersede the earlier software mandated by the 2022 directive.
FAA AD 2020-05-11: COMPLIANT — all 87 aircraft (pre-service compliance Nov 2020). All original compliance requirements met and maintained.

The 737 MAX airworthiness status is governed by FAA Airworthiness Directives under 14 CFR Part 39. Operators of Boeing 737-8 and 737-9 aircraft are legally required to comply with all applicable ADs, and non-compliance results in the aircraft being considered unairworthy for operation under FAA jurisdiction.
FAA AD 2024-01-02 (effective February 15, 2024).
No. AD 2020-05-11 is a previous airworthiness directive that is superseded.

The FAA issued an Emergency Order of Airworthiness on March 13, 2019, grounding all 737-8 and 737-9 aircraft. The FAA then issued AD 2020-05-11 in May 2020, establishing return-to-service requirements. 14 CFR Part 39 governs the process by which the FAA addresses unsafe conditions in aircraft, aircraft engines, propellers, and appliances through the issuance of Airworthiness Directives. The specific directive that references this part is § 39.11 of the Code of Federal Regulations, which mandates operator compliance with these directives.
The EASA directive covers the Airbus A320neo family, specifically the A320-200N and A321-271N models.
